metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix `classify_pair`'s definition to iterate the correct gate-output keys (`blocking`, `advisory`, `overflow`, `indeterminate`, `dropped`) instead of the non-existent `verdict_dict['findings']` key, and specify which of these lists are searched for target-family findings (e.g., whether `overflow` findings count toward detection).
evidence: Plan: 'a pure helper `classify_pair(verdict_dict, target_criteria)` applies the rules below, where a **target-family finding** is defined precisely as any finding in `verdict_dict["findings"]`'

orchestrator.py:531-544 shows the actual return dict keys: `verdict, ticket_id, ticket_type, blocking, advisory, coaching, overflow, indeterminate, dropped, coverage, runner, model` — no `findings` key
The schema (plan_review_verdict.schema.json) likewise has no `findings` property
findings.finding: `classify_pair`'s definition references `verdict_dict['findings']` — a key that does not exist in the actual `review_plan` return dict, which has `blocking`, `advisory`, `coaching`, `overflow`, `indeterminate`, `dropped` but NO `'findings'` top-level key (confirmed in orchestrator.py:531-544). An implementer following the plan as written will get a `KeyError` or empty result on every run.
findings.location: ## What — AMBIGUOUS / INDETERMINATE verdict handling section; `classify_pair` definition
scenarios: When `run_e2_benchmark.py` calls `classify_pair(verdict, target_criteria)`, accessing `verdict['findings']` will raise `KeyError` or return empty, making all mutants appear undetected and producing a spuriously clean baseline.
The golden tests in test_e2_counting.py will pass only if they use synthetic dicts that also define a `findings` key — which would not catch the actual bug when run against live gate output.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the `verdict_dict['findings']` reference with explicit iteration over `verdict_dict.get('blocking', []) + verdict_dict.get('advisory', []) + verdict_dict.get('indeterminate', []) + verdict_dict.get('dropped', []) + verdict_dict.get('overflow', [])`, and clarify in the counting rule whether overflow-capped advisory findings count as detected (since advisory-first discipline says advisory counts).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace all semicolons in the AC7 shell snippet with `&&` so that a non-zero exit from `--self-check-isolation` propagates and prevents the ISOLATION-OK echo: `H0=$(git -C .tickets-tracker rev-parse HEAD) && .venv/bin/python ... --self-check-isolation && H1=$(git -C .tickets-tracker rev-parse HEAD) && [ "$H0" = "$H1" ] && echo ISOLATION-OK`.
evidence: Command: 'H0=$(git -C .tickets-tracker rev-parse HEAD); .venv/bin/python ... --self-check-isolation; H1=$(git -C .tickets-tracker rev-parse HEAD); [ "$H0" = "$H1" ] && echo ISOLATION-OK'
All statements joined by `;` — failure of `--self-check-isolation` does not abort the sequence
If the script exits non-zero (assertion fails and aborts before any rebar edit), HEAD is still unchanged, so `[ "$H0" = "$H1" ]` passes and ISOLATION-OK is echoed despite the self-check failure
findings.finding: The AC7 proving command uses semicolons to separate all statements, so a non-zero exit from `run_e2_benchmark.py --self-check-isolation` does not stop execution; the HEAD-comparison still runs and prints ISOLATION-OK as long as HEAD was not changed — which it won't be if the script aborted early. The isolation self-check failure is silently swallowed.
findings.location: AC7 proving command (shell snippet)
scenarios: The standalone-clone assertion fails (e.g., .git is a file, not a directory) and the script exits 1, but the prove command still prints ISOLATION-OK, giving a false green on the most critical safety check.
findings.suggested_fix:
```bash
H0=$(git -C .tickets-tracker rev-parse HEAD) && \
.venv/bin/python docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/harnesses/run_e2_benchmark.py --self-check-isolation && \
H1=$(git -C .tickets-tracker rev-parse HEAD) && \
[ "$H0" = "$H1" ] && echo ISOLATION-OK
```

findings.finding: No AC verifies that the gate actually received and processed the MUTATED plan text (i.e., that `REBAR_TRACKER_DIR` routing to the clone is functioning). The isolation AC (AC 7) checks only that the real store's HEAD is unchanged after a run — it does not confirm that the mutant's modified description was the input to `review_plan`. A broken routing (e.g., `REBAR_TRACKER_DIR` silently ignored, gate falling back to real store) would produce systematically low detection rates indistinguishable from 'the gate legitimately cannot catch this class,' silently producing an invalid baseline without triggering any AC failure.
findings.location: ## What — Gate-execution mechanism; Acceptance Criteria — AC 4 and AC 7
findings.suggested_fix: Add to `run_e2_benchmark.py`'s `run_validity` logic (or as a pre-flight check in `--self-check-isolation`): select the class-2 or class-3 smoke-test pair whose baseline detection is expected 'partial' (not near-zero), run it, and assert `mutant_verdict != control_verdict`. Record this check in `e2_run_validity.json` as `{routing_smoke_test: {mutant_verdict, control_verdict, routing_confirmed: bool}}`. Fail the run non-zero if `routing_confirmed` is False.

findings.finding: The crash-resumable behavior of `run_e2_benchmark.py` ('journals raw verdicts incrementally (`e2_verdicts.jsonl`, crash-resumable)') is a described deliverable in Scope with no covering AC. No prove-command or test asserts that a partial `e2_verdicts.jsonl` allows the harness to skip already-completed rows on re-run.
findings.location: Scope: `run_e2_benchmark.py` description; AC4, AC5, AC6, AC7 (none covers crash-resume)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace `passes.py:269,514` with the actual locus of agentic code-grounding (the `RunnerAgentStep(repo_root=REAL_REPO_CHECKOUT)` injection in `gate_dispatch.py` and `cfg.repo_path` propagation) so the cited lines match the described mechanism.
evidence: passes.py:269: `prompt = prompts.get_prompt(prompt_id, repo_root=cfg.repo_path)` — this is prompt resolution, not code-grounding
passes.py:514: `prompt = prompts.get_prompt('plan-review-isf-summarizer', repo_root=cfg.repo_path)` — this is an ISF summarizer prompt fetch
The actual agentic code-grounding occurs via the workflow RunnerAgentStep (gate_dispatch.py:169: `agent_runner=RunnerAgentStep(runner=runner_sel, repo_root=repo_root, config=cfg)`)
registry.py:66 CODEBASE_GROUNDED = frozenset({'E4', 'G1G2', 'A1', 'G6'}) — these are the code-grounding criteria, routed agentic by the workflow, not by passes.py:269/514
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'the agentic finders (`passes.py:269,514`) read the REAL modules' to justify that class-1 (absent capability) and class-4 (unnecessary mechanism) code-grounding is real. But passes.py:269 is `prompts.get_prompt(prompt_id, repo_root=cfg.repo_path)` (prompt-template resolution) and passes.py:514 is `prompts.get_prompt('plan-review-isf-summarizer', ...)` (an ISF-summarizer prompt fetch). Neither is an agentic code-grounding call. The actual mechanism by which agentic criteria (E4, G1G2, A1, G6 in the AGENT_TIER frozenset) read the real code is through the workflow's RunnerAgentStep / gate_dispatch.py's agent_runner — not these specific pass lines.
findings.location: ## What — Gate-execution mechanism paragraph: '`source="local"` sets no code ContextVar, so `ctx.repo_root = resolve_code_root(repo_root=REAL_REPO_CHECKOUT)` and `cfg.repo_path` = the real checkout; the DET-floor absence probe (`det_floor.py:345,392`) and the agentic finders (`passes.py:269,514`) read the REAL modules'
scenarios: An implementer of `run_e2_benchmark.py` reads the cited lines to understand how code-grounding reaches the real checkout and finds unrelated prompt-resolution code, creating confusion about where the grounding actually happens and whether it's correctly wired.
A reviewer auditing the isolation correctness of code-grounding vs. ticket-store separation uses these citations as the grounding locus and reaches wrong conclusions about the data flow.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the parenthetical with the correct mechanism citations: '…the DET-floor absence probe (`det_floor.py:345,392`) and the agentic criteria (CODEBASE_GROUNDED: E4/G1G2/A1/G6) read code via `cfg.repo_path` = REAL_REPO_CHECKOUT (set by `apply_handle` in `gate_source.py:120-126` for local mode, or via `RunnerAgentStep(repo_root=repo_root, config=cfg)` in `gate_dispatch.py:169`).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the drift-refresh bypass attribution: replace '`force=True` (skip idempotent-reuse / drift-refresh)' with '`sign=False` (skips both idempotent-reuse and drift-refresh, since both are gated on `if sign...`; `force=True` additionally skips idempotent-reuse when sign=True) — `__init__.py:508,517`'.
evidence: __init__.py:508: `if sign and not force:` — force DOES gate idempotent-reuse bypass
__init__.py:517: `if sign:` — drift-refresh check has NO `not force` guard; force=True does not bypass it
__init__.py:507 comment: '`force` bypasses BOTH this skip and the drift-refresh' — this inline comment is itself inaccurate per the code at line 517
With sign=False (the plan's actual call), both lines 508 and 517 evaluate to False, so both are skipped — but the causal mechanism is sign=False, not force=True
findings.finding: The plan asserts '`force=True` (skip idempotent-reuse / drift-refresh so every mutant runs fresh)' citing `__init__.py:508-518`. But at line 517, drift-refresh is gated by `if sign:` (no `not force` guard), meaning `force=True` alone does NOT bypass drift-refresh. The plan's own mechanism uses `sign=False`, which does bypass drift-refresh (because `if sign:` is False). The inline code comment at line 507 says 'force bypasses BOTH' but the code contradicts this for `sign=True, force=True`. The plan's practical outcome is correct (sign=False skips drift-refresh), but the stated reason ('force=True bypasses drift-refresh') is technically wrong and could cause a future caller using `sign=True, force=True` to believe drift-refresh is bypassed when it is not.
findings.location: ## What — Gate-execution mechanism: 'Non-mutating flags: `sign=False` (no attestation), `emit_sidecar=False` (no `REVIEW_RESULT` write — critical so E2 does not pollute E1/R7's sidecar corpus), `force=True` (skip idempotent-reuse / drift-refresh so every mutant runs fresh) — `__init__.py:508-518,570-587`'
scenarios: An implementer reading the plan and the cited code lines sees `force=True` credited for bypassing drift-refresh, looks at line 517 (`if sign:`), and is confused — the lines don't match the claim, eroding confidence in the plan's accuracy.

A future harness variant using `sign=True, force=True` (e.g., to test a signed-review scenario) would not bypass drift-refresh, silently reusing a stale attestation from a prior run on the mutant store.
findings.suggested_fix: Update the Non-mutating flags note to: '`sign=False` (skips signing, idempotent-reuse, and drift-refresh — all three gated on `sign`), `emit_sidecar=False` (no REVIEW_RESULT write), `force=True` (additionally prevents idempotent-reuse when sign=True, defensive belt-and-suspenders) — `__init__.py:508,517,570`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether `overflow`-capped advisory findings count toward detection in `classify_pair`, and ensure this rule is consistently reflected in both the harness implementation and the golden test fixtures.
evidence: Plan: 'detected (mutant, recall): a target-family finding surfaced with `decision ∈ {block, advisory}` — advisory counts'
orchestrator.py:444-447: `'surfaced': advisory[:advisory_cap], 'overflow': advisory[advisory_cap:]` — the live gate splits advisory findings by cap
plan_review_verdict.schema.json: `advisory` is 'capped at the config top-N'; `overflow` is 'Advisory findings beyond the cap (sidecar-only)'
findings.finding: The plan says advisory findings count for detection ('advisory counts, because the proposed criteria ship advisory-first') but the live gate splits advisory findings into `advisory` (within the advisory cap) and `overflow` (beyond the cap, sidecar-only). The plan never specifies whether an `overflow` finding — which has `decision='advisory'` but was demoted past the cap — counts as detected. This is a co-located schema/decision-rule gap: the processing protocol (advisory counts) is stated without reference to the advisory-cap split that determines which advisory findings are accessible.
findings.location: ## What — AMBIGUOUS / INDETERMINATE verdict handling section; 'detected (mutant, recall)' rule
scenarios: A class-1 mutant whose target-family finding ranks just outside the advisory cap lands only in `overflow`. If classify_pair only scans `advisory`, the mutant is scored as undetected — underestimating recall for the baseline.
Conversely, if both `advisory` and `overflow` are scanned, a run that produces a high-priority overflow finding correctly counts as detected — the intent is unclear.
findings.suggested_fix: Given the plan's advisory-first intent, state explicitly: 'overflow findings (advisory findings beyond the cap) also count as detected — `classify_pair` scans `blocking + advisory + overflow + indeterminate` for target-family findings with `decision ∈ {block, advisory}`'. Update the golden tests to include an overflow-advisory fixture.

findings.finding: The plan describes `det_floor.py:345,392` as 'the DET-floor absence probe' that 'makes class-1 (absent capability) and class-4 (unnecessary mechanism) grounding real.' Reading the file: line 345 is `grounding.refute_absence({'kind': 'file', 'name': ref}, repo_root=ctx.repo_root)` (P2 file-path resolution — checks whether a file a plan REFERENCES exists) and line 392 is `grounding.refute_absence({'kind': 'dependency', 'name': pkg}, ...)` (P3 package existence). Neither performs absent-capability probing or unnecessary-mechanism detection. Class-1 (absent asserted capability) and class-4 (unnecessary mechanism) are LLM-driven via E4, G1G2, G6, and the agentic verifier — not these DET-floor file/package probes.
findings.location: ## What — Gate-execution mechanism section: 'the DET-floor absence probe (`det_floor.py:345,392`) … makes class-1 (absent capability) and class-4 (unnecessary mechanism) grounding real'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix the AC1 proving command to add an explicit class-5 minimum assertion, e.g. append `and m['5']>=5` to the existing count assert so the ≥5 floor is enforced alongside the ≥10 floor for classes 1–4.
evidence: AC text: 'the proving command below asserts ≥10 for classes 1–4 and the recorded N for class 5'
Command: `all(m[k]>=10 for k in '1234')` — iterates '1','2','3','4' only
Command: `set(m)==set(ctl)=={'1','2','3','4','5'}` — asserts existence, not count ≥ 5 for class 5
Degradation rule: 'run class 5 at the available N≥5' — floor of 5 is specified in ## What but not enforced by the prove command
findings.finding: The AC1 proving command does not assert the class-5 minimum count of ≥5; it only asserts that class '5' exists in the Counter (via `set(m)=={'1','2','3','4','5'}`), while `all(m[k]>=10 for k in '1234')` iterates only characters '1','2','3','4', silently skipping the ≥5 floor for class 5. The AC text claims 'the proving command asserts ≥10 for classes 1–4 and the recorded N for class 5' but no such assertion for the recorded N of class 5 is present in the command.
findings.location: AC1 proving command (the `python3 -c` one-liner)
scenarios: Corpus ships with only 3 class-5 mutants (below the ≥5 floor); the proving command passes because class '5' exists in the Counter, masking the shortfall.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the assert to: `assert all(m[k]>=10 for k in '1234') and m['5']>=5, (dict(m),dict(ctl))`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the class-5 threshold representation: either (a) express it as a rate in the threshold table ('≥70% detection') matching the degradation ladder language, or (b) add a 'rate_fallback' field to the e2_thresholds.json schema (and the AC #3 structure check) that the harness uses when N<10, so the count vs. rate fallback is unambiguous at implementation time.
evidence: Threshold table: 'Class 5 | ≥7/10 flagged | ≤1/10 blocking-flagged'
Degradation ladder: 'drop to the available N≥5, keep the thresholds as RATES (≥70% detection, ≤10% blocking-FP)'
AC #1 prove command: 'm["5"]>=5' (not >=10)
e2_thresholds.json structure check (AC #3) only verifies presence of 'detection_min' and 'blocking_fp_max' — no mechanism specified to distinguish count vs. rate fallback in the JSON schema
findings.finding: The threshold table in ## What pins class 5's detection target as '≥7/10 flagged' (an absolute count requiring N=10 mutants), but the class-5 degradation ladder in the same section explicitly allows class 5 to run at N≥5 with 'rate' thresholds (≥70%), and AC #1's prove command only asserts m['5']>=5. At N=5, the count threshold '≥7/10' is literally unachievable, creating a direct contradiction between the threshold table and the degradation ladder that would leave an implementer unable to determine whether a class-5 run at N=7 with 5 detections passes (5/7 ≈ 71% ≥ 70% rate, but 5 < 7 absolute count).
findings.location: ## What — Pre-registered per-class thresholds table (class 5 row) vs. Degradation ladder subsection
scenarios: Class 5 runs at N=7 (plausible given '12 clean bases' with inspection losses); implementer cannot determine if '5 detections' passes (5/7=71%≥70% rate) or fails (5<7 absolute count).
findings.suggested_fix: Change the threshold table class-5 row from '≥7/10 flagged' to '≥70% flagged (≥7/10 at N=10; rate applies when N<10)' and add a `rate_fallback: true` field to class 5's entry in e2_thresholds.json, tested in the AC #3 structure check.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the crash-resume deduplication strategy in `run_e2_benchmark.py`: on startup, read already-journaled `pair_id`s from `e2_verdicts.jsonl` and skip those pairs; and decide whether the tmp clone is recreated fresh each run or reused (and if reused, how already-mutated tickets are handled).
evidence: 'journals raw verdicts incrementally (e2_verdicts.jsonl, crash-resumable)' — no deduplication or skip-if-already-present logic described
The baseline computation reads from e2_verdicts.jsonl; no mention of a set-difference / already-seen pair_id guard on resume
On crash-resume the tmp mutant store clone may already have mutations committed; the plan does not say whether the clone is recreated fresh or reused from the crashed state
findings.finding: The crash-resume mechanism (`e2_verdicts.jsonl` is 'journaled incrementally') does not specify how duplicate rows are prevented on resume. If a run crashes mid-batch and is restarted, pairs that were already processed and appended to the JSONL will be re-run and appended again, causing `e2_baseline.json` to be computed over duplicate rows — silently inflating detected/FP counts and invalidating the baseline.
findings.location: ## Scope — run_e2_benchmark.py entry ('journals raw verdicts incrementally (e2_verdicts.jsonl, crash-resumable)')
scenarios: Run crashes after 40 of 100 pairs; user reruns; 40 pairs are re-processed and appended; e2_baseline.json is computed over 140 rows instead of 100, double-counting the first 40
Clone is reused from crashed state; rebar edit is re-applied over an already-mutated ticket, producing a doubly-mutated plan text whose gate result is attributed to the wrong mutation
findings.suggested_fix: On harness startup: load the set of already-processed `pair_id`s from `e2_verdicts.jsonl` (if it exists); skip any pair whose `pair_id` is already in that set. Recreate the tmp clone fresh on every (re)start (a `git clone --local` of a local repo is fast) to avoid doubly-mutated ticket state; the skip-set ensures already-journaled pairs are not re-run.

evidence: description is 29329 chars (> 24000)
findings.finding: Oversize signals suggest this unit may be too large for one session.
findings.suggested_fix: Split into smaller child tickets, each a coherent single outcome.

evidence: vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `--verify-clean` flag contract to the `assemble_e2_mutants.py` scope bullet, specifying that it cross-checks every `source_ticket_id` in the written corpus against its `outcome_corpus.jsonl` row for the 5-condition filter, exits 0 on full pass, and exits non-zero naming the first violating ticket.
evidence: Scope: '--dry-run prints the selected base pool without writing' — no mention of --verify-clean
AC2: 'python3 docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/harnesses/assemble_e2_mutants.py --dry-run --verify-clean && echo FILTER-OK'

AC2 prose: 'exits 0 only if every selected base ticket's corpus row passes the 5-condition filter; non-zero names the first violating ticket' — this describes --verify-clean's behavior but only in the AC, not in the scope definition of the script
findings.finding: The `--verify-clean` flag is referenced in AC2's proving command (`assemble_e2_mutants.py --dry-run --verify-clean`) but the scope bullet for `assemble_e2_mutants.py` only mentions `--dry-run prints the selected base pool without writing` — `--verify-clean`'s contract (what it checks, what exit code it emits, what it prints on violation) is not specified anywhere in the plan.
findings.location: ## Scope / assemble_e2_mutants.py bullet; AC2 proving command
scenarios: Executor implements --dry-run correctly but omits --verify-clean entirely, making AC2 fail at the flag-parsing step rather than at a filter violation.
Executor implements --verify-clean with different semantics (e.g., validates only a sample) because the scope bullet doesn't define it.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the scope bullet: "`--verify-clean` (combinable with `--dry-run`): after selecting bases, asserts every selected ticket's `outcome_corpus.jsonl` row satisfies all five filter conditions; exits 0 on full pass, exits 1 and prints the first violating ticket id otherwise."

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `--self-check-isolation` mode to the `run_e2_benchmark.py` scope bullet, specifying that it creates the tmp clone, asserts `os.path.isdir(TMP/.git)`, exits 0 if the assertion holds and the clone is confirmed standalone, and exits non-zero (with a diagnostic message) if the assertion fails — without running any gate calls.
evidence: Scope for run_e2_benchmark.py mentions the standalone-clone assertion inline ('The harness ASSERTS the clone is standalone ... BEFORE any injection') but does not describe a --self-check-isolation CLI mode
AC7 proving command: '.venv/bin/python docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/harnesses/run_e2_benchmark.py --self-check-isolation'
AC7 prose: 'asserts os.path.isdir(.git) before any rebar edit injection' — behavior described only in ## What, not as a named CLI mode in scope
findings.finding: The `--self-check-isolation` mode is referenced in AC7's proving command (`run_e2_benchmark.py --self-check-isolation`) but the scope bullet for `run_e2_benchmark.py` does not mention this flag or define its behavior (what it asserts, what it outputs, what exit code it returns on assertion failure).
findings.location: ## Scope / run_e2_benchmark.py bullet; AC7 proving command
scenarios: Executor implements the harness without a --self-check-isolation flag, so AC7's prove command fails at argument parsing.
Executor implements the flag but with different behavior (e.g., only prints a message, doesn't exit non-zero on failure), making the HEAD-comparison in AC7 meaningless.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the run_e2_benchmark.py scope bullet: "`--self-check-isolation`: creates the tmp mutant store clone, asserts `os.path.isdir(TMP_MUTANT_STORE/.git)` (exits 1 with error message if this assertion fails), then exits 0 — does NOT inject mutants or run gate calls; used by the isolation AC prove step."

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the `--verify-clean` flag for `assemble_e2_mutants.py` in the Scope section: specify what it checks (e.g., cross-validates each selected `source_ticket_id` against the 5-condition corpus-row filter) and what constitutes a non-zero exit (first violating ticket id printed to stderr).
evidence: Scope: '`--dry-run` prints the selected base pool without writing'
AC2: `python3 docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/harnesses/assemble_e2_mutants.py --dry-run --verify-clean && echo FILTER-OK`
No definition of `--verify-clean` anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The AC2 proving command invokes `assemble_e2_mutants.py --dry-run --verify-clean`, but the Scope section's description of that script only mentions `--dry-run` ('`--dry-run` prints the selected base pool without writing'). The `--verify-clean` flag is never defined: its behavior, what it checks, and what constitutes a non-zero exit are unspecified.
findings.location: AC2 proving command; Scope — `assemble_e2_mutants.py` description
scenarios: An implementer writing `assemble_e2_mutants.py` must invent the semantics of `--verify-clean` without guidance, potentially producing a flag that exits 0 unconditionally and gives false FILTER-OK.
A reviewer validating AC2 cannot independently confirm what the flag is testing.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `--verify-clean` (exits 0 only if every selected base's corpus row passes the 5-condition filter, names first violating ticket on non-zero) and `--self-check-isolation` (runs a single-pair dry-run asserting isdir(.git) before injection and verifying no mutation commits reach the real store) to their respective Scope descriptions.
evidence: Scope for assemble_e2_mutants.py: '`--dry-run` prints the selected base pool without writing' — no `--verify-clean` mentioned
Scope for run_e2_benchmark.py: describes the clone mechanism and gate invocation, but no `--self-check-isolation` flag mentioned
AC #2 prove: 'python3 docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/harnesses/assemble_e2_mutants.py --dry-run --verify-clean && echo FILTER-OK'
AC #7 prove: '.venv/bin/python docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/harnesses/run_e2_benchmark.py --self-check-isolation'
findings.finding: AC #2's prove command invokes `assemble_e2_mutants.py --dry-run --verify-clean`, and AC #7's prove command invokes `run_e2_benchmark.py --self-check-isolation`, but neither the `--verify-clean` flag for assemble_e2_mutants.py nor the `--self-check-isolation` flag for run_e2_benchmark.py is described anywhere in the Scope section for those scripts. The verify steps reference CLI behavior that an implementer has no specification for.
findings.location: ## Scope (assemble_e2_mutants.py description; run_e2_benchmark.py description) vs. AC #2 and AC #7 prove commands
scenarios: Implementer writes assemble_e2_mutants.py with only --dry-run; CI fails AC #2 because --verify-clean is unrecognized.
Implementer writes run_e2_benchmark.py without --self-check-isolation mode; AC #7 isolation proof cannot execute.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope bullet for assemble_e2_mutants.py, append: '`--verify-clean` (may be combined with `--dry-run`): cross-checks each selected base ticket's corpus row against the 5-condition filter and exits non-zero naming the first violating ticket.' In the Scope bullet for run_e2_benchmark.py, append: '`--self-check-isolation`: runs a minimal single-pair gate call, asserts `os.path.isdir(TMP_MUTANT_STORE/.git)` before injection, verifies the real `.tickets-tracker` HEAD is unchanged afterward, and exits 0 or non-zero accordingly.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Require `run_e2_benchmark.py` to explicitly read `indeterminate_cap` from `e2_thresholds.json` and pass it as a keyword argument to `run_validity(…, indeterminate_cap=t['indeterminate_cap'])`, and update the `emit_baseline` seam signature / integration test fixture to exercise this path — eliminating the hardcoded-default as an active code path.
evidence: Plan defines `run_validity(per_class_verdicts, indeterminate_cap=0.20)` with a hardcoded default
Plan also states `'indeterminate_cap' in t` must hold in e2_thresholds.json
Scope says harness 'Reads its targets from runs/e2_thresholds.json' but does not specify that indeterminate_cap is extracted and forwarded to run_validity
If the JSON cap is changed (e.g., to 0.15) but the harness still calls run_validity() without the kwarg, the default 0.20 silently wins
findings.finding: The `indeterminate_cap=0.20` value appears both as a hardcoded default parameter in `run_validity(per_class_verdicts, indeterminate_cap=0.20)` AND as a field (`indeterminate_cap`) in the pre-registered `runs/e2_thresholds.json`. The plan states the harness 'Reads its targets from `runs/e2_thresholds.json`' but never explicitly requires the harness to pass the JSON's `indeterminate_cap` value to `run_validity` — leaving open the possibility that the function default silently shadows the JSON value if a future author changes only the JSON.
findings.location: ## What — AMBIGUOUS / INDETERMINATE verdict handling; Scope — `run_e2_benchmark.py` bullet; AC for run-validity guard
scenarios: A future calibration lowers the cap in the JSON to be stricter; the harness silently ignores it because run_validity uses its default; a high-abstain run is incorrectly accepted.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope bullet for `run_e2_benchmark.py`, add: 'reads `indeterminate_cap` from `e2_thresholds.json` and passes it explicitly to `run_validity` and `emit_baseline`'. In `e2_counting.py`, keep the default only as documentation (or remove it and require the caller to supply the value), so the JSON is the single source of truth.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the precedence rule for `indeterminate_cap`: state whether `run_e2_benchmark.py` passes the JSON-loaded value explicitly to `run_validity` (overriding the Python default) or whether the Python default is canonical and the JSON entry is advisory-only.
evidence: Plan: 'a second pure helper `run_validity(per_class_verdicts, indeterminate_cap=0.20)` computes, per class, the INDETERMINATE fraction'
Plan: '`e2_thresholds.json` pins the per-class detection target + blocking-FP bound for all 5 classes and the INDETERMINATE run-validity cap'
Plan: '`run_e2_benchmark.py` … Reads its targets from `runs/e2_thresholds.json`'
findings.finding: The `indeterminate_cap` has two declared sources of truth with no stated precedence: (1) it is hardcoded as a Python default parameter `run_validity(per_class_verdicts, indeterminate_cap=0.20)`, AND (2) it is stored as a field in `runs/e2_thresholds.json`, which the harness 'Reads its targets from'. If the JSON value and the Python default diverge (e.g., the JSON is updated for a follow-on experiment), the plan gives no rule for which wins — a decision rule without co-located protocol.
findings.location: ## What — Run-validity guard paragraph; Scope — `run_e2_benchmark.py` description
scenarios: A future author edits `e2_thresholds.json` to cap=0.15 for a stricter re-run, but the Python default remains 0.20; the harness uses the wrong cap silently.

The golden tests in test_e2_counting.py call `run_validity` with no explicit cap argument, picking up the Python default, while the live harness reads the cap from JSON — the tests may not exercise the actual operational configuration.
findings.suggested_fix: State: '`run_e2_benchmark.py` loads `indeterminate_cap` from `e2_thresholds.json` and passes it explicitly to `run_validity(per_class_verdicts, indeterminate_cap=loaded_cap)`, making the JSON the single source of truth; the Python default of 0.20 is a fallback for tests that do not supply a cap.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the supported CLI flags (`--self-check-isolation`, and any other modes such as `--dry-run` or `--class`) to the `run_e2_benchmark.py` Scope bullet, mirroring the pattern used for `assemble_e2_mutants.py`.

--dry-run and --verify-clean flags for assemble_e2_mutants.py ARE mentioned in its Scope bullet
--self-check-isolation appears only in the AC prove command: 'run_e2_benchmark.py --self-check-isolation'
A reader of the Scope section alone cannot discover what modes/flags the benchmark harness supports
findings.finding: The `run_e2_benchmark.py` Scope bullet describes the script's behaviour (clone creation, gate invocation, output files) but does not enumerate its CLI flags. The `--self-check-isolation` flag — the only externally-invokable mode beyond a normal run — appears solely in the AC prove command, leaving the script's user-facing interface partially undocumented in the plan.
findings.location: ## Scope — `run_e2_benchmark.py` bullet; AC: 'Prove BOTH the standalone-clone assertion…'
scenarios: A developer reading the Scope section to understand how to invoke run_e2_benchmark.py does not know --self-check-isolation exists; they may re-implement or omit the isolation check.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the run_e2_benchmark.py Scope bullet: '`--self-check-isolation` asserts the tmp mutant store's `.git` is a directory (not a file-pointer) and exits 0/non-zero without running full benchmark; `--class <N>` (optional) limits a run to one defect class for faster iteration.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in Scope how `e2_counting.py` (a non-package harness script) is imported by the test files — either add `tests/unit/experiments/__init__.py` to scope and use path-based import via `importlib.util.spec_from_file_location` (matching the `test_gate_eval_classifier.py` pattern), or explain an alternative (e.g., make `e2_counting.py` importable via `sys.path` manipulation in a `conftest.py`).
evidence: Plan scope: 'tests/unit/experiments/test_e2_counting.py — CI-collectable golden test for the two pure helpers in e2_counting.py'
Harness is at docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/harnesses/e2_counting.py — not a Python package
Existing precedent: test_gate_eval_classifier.py:15-22 uses importlib.util.spec_from_file_location to load non-package harness scripts
ABSENCE: no __init__.py for tests/unit/experiments/ mentioned in Scope; existing subdirs tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/ and tests/unit/workflow/ both have __init__.py
findings.finding: The plan creates `tests/unit/experiments/test_e2_counting.py` and `tests/unit/experiments/test_e2_benchmark.py` in a new `tests/unit/experiments/` subdirectory, but does not specify a `__init__.py` for that directory nor the import mechanism for `e2_counting.py` (a non-package script at `docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/harnesses/e2_counting.py`). All existing `tests/unit/` subdirs with tests (`rebar_reconciler/`, `workflow/`) have `__init__.py`. The pattern for importing non-package harness scripts in this repo is `importlib.util.spec_from_file_location` (see `test_gate_eval_classifier.py:15-22`), but the plan says e2_counting is simply 'imported by … the unit test' without specifying this loading mechanism — if the implementer writes a bare `import e2_counting`, it will fail at collection.
findings.location: ## Scope — `tests/unit/experiments/test_e2_counting.py` and `test_e2_benchmark.py`; AC 'Prove: .venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/unit/experiments/test_e2_counting.py'
scenarios: Implementer writes `from e2_counting import classify_pair` or `import e2_counting` directly — this raises ModuleNotFoundError at pytest collection time since the harness directory is not on sys.path.
CI fails on `pytest tests/unit/experiments/test_e2_counting.py` with an import error, blocking the AC prove command.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `tests/unit/experiments/__init__.py` (empty) and `tests/unit/experiments/conftest.py` to the Scope list; in the test files use the established repo pattern: load `e2_counting` via `importlib.util.spec_from_file_location('e2_counting', Path(__file__).parents[3] / 'docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/harnesses/e2_counting.py')` (same pattern as `test_gate_eval_classifier.py:15-22`).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add per-call timeout, Anthropic API rate-limit backoff (e.g. exponential retry with cap), and a rough cost estimate / budget guard to `run_e2_benchmark.py`'s gate-invocation loop.
evidence: Plan states ≥10 mutants per class for 5 classes (~100 gate calls) but specifies no rate-limit handling, no per-call timeout, and no cost estimate
'review_plan(ticket_id, source="local", repo_root=REAL_REPO_CHECKOUT, sign=False, emit_sidecar=False, force=True)' — called per mutant/control with no mention of retry, backoff, or concurrency cap
The existing harnesses are noted to 'hand-roll anthropic.messages.create' — the production gate similarly calls the Anthropic API; no budget guard is specified
findings.finding: The benchmark harness runs ~100+ LLM-backed `review_plan` calls with no rate-limit handling, no per-call timeout, and no cost estimate. Each production gate call invokes the Anthropic API (agentic passes, DET floor); at stated scale (≥10 mutants + ≥10 controls × 5 classes = ≥100 calls) an API rate-limit error or a transient spike in per-call latency will either silently stall the run or surface as an unhandled exception, and there is no stated budget guard against runaway spend.
findings.location: ## What — Gate-execution mechanism; run_e2_benchmark.py scope entry
scenarios: Anthropic API returns a 429 rate-limit mid-batch; the harness crashes, the JSONL is partially written, and the resume path (see T5b finding) may re-run already-completed pairs
Per-call latency spikes cause the full run to take hours with no progress indicator or timeout, blocking CI
~100 calls at $0.01–0.05 per call accumulates meaningful cost with no stated budget ceiling
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap each `review_plan(...)` call in a retry loop (e.g. `tenacity.retry` with `wait_exponential`, `stop_after_attempt(3)`, catching `anthropic.RateLimitError` and `anthropic.APITimeoutError`); add a `--timeout-per-call N` CLI flag (default 120 s); and document the expected cost per full run (e.g. '~100 calls × median $X = $Y') in the README E2 section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the RETIRE/cleanup path for TMP_MUTANT_STORE in `run_e2_benchmark.py`: use a `tempfile.mkdtemp`-backed context manager (or equivalent `try/finally` with `shutil.rmtree`) to guarantee the clone is removed on both success and crash.
evidence: 'The harness therefore creates the mutant store with git clone --local --branch tickets <REAL_REPO_CHECKOUT> <TMP_MUTANT_STORE>' — no corresponding cleanup step
No `finally` / `atexit` / `tempfile.TemporaryDirectory` or explicit `shutil.rmtree` mentioned
RETIRE/cleanup is absent from the lifecycle description
findings.finding: The TMP_MUTANT_STORE git clone (created by `git clone --local --branch tickets`) has no defined RETIRE/cleanup path. The plan specifies CREATE (git clone) and UPDATE (rebar edit injections) but never says when or how the temporary clone is removed — neither on successful completion nor on crash.
findings.location: ## What — Gate-execution mechanism ('Mutant store = an INDEPENDENT git clone'); ## Scope — run_e2_benchmark.py
scenarios: Each benchmark run (or re-run after a crash) leaves a multi-hundred-MB git clone in the filesystem; repeated runs accumulate multiple clones if the tmp path is timestamped or randomly named
A crash leaves the clone in a partially-mutated state; a subsequent run, if it reuses the same path, reads stale mutation commits
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the clone lifecycle in a `contextlib.contextmanager` that calls `git clone --local ...` on enter and `shutil.rmtree(TMP_MUTANT_STORE, ignore_errors=True)` on exit (both normal and exception paths). Alternatively, use `tempfile.TemporaryDirectory()` as the context manager and pass its `.name` as the clone target.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Eliminate the dual-source for `indeterminate_cap`: either remove the Python default parameter (force callers to always pass the value read from `e2_thresholds.json`) or make the pure helper accept only a mandatory positional argument, and update `test_e2_counting.py` to pass the threshold explicitly so the test reflects what the harness actually configures.
evidence: Plan text: 'a second pure helper `run_validity(per_class_verdicts, indeterminate_cap=0.20)` computes, per class, the INDETERMINATE fraction over mutants+controls and `valid = frac <= cap` (0.20 = 2.5× the 8% historical base rate)'
Plan text: '`runs/e2_thresholds.json` pins the per-class detection target + blocking-FP bound for all 5 classes and the INDETERMINATE run-validity cap'
Plan text (Scope): 'Reads its targets from `runs/e2_thresholds.json`' (the harness reads JSON, but the pure helper has a Python default)

AC5: 'golden-tested with synthetic verdict dicts — including a fixture whose class has >20% INDETERMINATE that the test asserts `run_validity` flags invalid' — this test calls the pure helper directly, using the Python default, not the JSON value
findings.finding: The `indeterminate_cap` has two authoritative sources: it is hardcoded as a Python default parameter `run_validity(per_class_verdicts, indeterminate_cap=0.20)` on the pure helper AND stored as `indeterminate_cap` in `runs/e2_thresholds.json` (which the harness reads). The AC golden test drives `run_validity` directly with synthetic dicts — it will use the Python default, not the JSON value — so if the threshold is ever adjusted in the JSON file, the unit tests silently continue testing the old default. This dual-source is a config-surface proliferation: one constant, two places.
findings.location: ## What — AMBIGUOUS / INDETERMINATE verdict handling paragraph (`run_validity(per_class_verdicts, indeterminate_cap=0.20)`); Scope — `e2_counting.py`; AC5 golden test; Scope — `runs/e2_thresholds.json`
scenarios: An R-story author changes `indeterminate_cap` in `e2_thresholds.json` to 0.15 for a tighter validity gate; the harness respects the new value, but the unit test still passes trivially against the Python default 0.20 — the golden test no longer validates the configured threshold.
The two values silently diverge, making it unclear which is authoritative when debugging a failing run.
findings.suggested_fix: Change `run_validity(per_class_verdicts, indeterminate_cap=0.20)` to `run_validity(per_class_verdicts, indeterminate_cap: float)` (no default), and update both the harness (which already reads it from JSON) and the test (pass `0.20` explicitly). This makes the test self-documenting and ensures any future threshold edit in the JSON is reflected in test expectations.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit constraint in `run_e2_benchmark.py`'s scope description (or the gate-execution mechanism paragraph) that mutant gate runs MUST be sequential within a process (no thread parallelism), OR specify a per-run isolation strategy (e.g., subprocess-per-mutant so each process has its own env) to prevent REBAR_TRACKER_DIR race conditions.
evidence: gate_source.py:104-117: gate_read_root only calls use_tickets_root for SOURCE_ATTESTED, not local mode
config.py:126-128: tracker_dir checks REBAR_TRACKER_DIR env var first (global process state)
plan: 'REBAR_TRACKER_DIR redirects assemble_context's show_ticket/list_tickets to the tmp mutant store'
The mechanism is correct for sequential runs, but the plan does not specify that mutant runs must be sequential (no parallelism constraint stated)
findings.finding: The plan's gate-execution mechanism states `review_plan(ticket_id, source='local', repo_root=REAL_REPO_CHECKOUT, sign=False, emit_sidecar=False, force=True)` and asserts that `REBAR_TRACKER_DIR=TMP_MUTANT_STORE` redirects all ticket reads. However, `review_plan` internally calls `gate_source.resolve_gate_handle(ref, source, repo_root)` and enters `gate_source.gate_read_root(handle)` — for `source='local'`, this context manager does NOT call `use_tickets_root()` (only called for `SOURCE_ATTESTED` in `gate_source.py:111-117`). This means `_active_tickets_root` ContextVar remains `None`. In `_assemble_context_uncached`, `tickets_root=current_tickets_root()` → `None` on the PlanContext. Downstream ticket reads in pass1.py (e.g., `_reads.show_ticket(tgt, repo_root=ctx.tickets_root)` at `pass1.py:319`) pass `None` as `repo_root` to `_reads.show_ticket`, which then resolves tracker via `config.tracker_dir(None)` → checks `REBAR_TRACKER_DIR` → correctly hits the mutant store. The mechanism works, BUT only because REBAR_TRACKER_DIR is an env-var override that takes precedence globally. The plan's stated reasoning ('`REBAR_TRACKER_DIR` wins verbatim over `repo_root` for ALL ticket reads') is correct — but it depends on env-var global state affecting every thread. If the harness runs mutants in parallel (e.g., a thread pool), all threads share the same process env, creating a race condition if `REBAR_TRACKER_DIR` is mutated between mutant runs.
findings.location: ## What — 'Gate-execution mechanism (verified)' section; `run_e2_benchmark.py` scope entry
scenarios: If `run_e2_benchmark.py` uses a thread pool to speed up the ≥100 gate runs (10+ mutants × 5 classes + controls), setting os.environ['REBAR_TRACKER_DIR'] in one thread affects all other threads simultaneously, causing cross-contamination between mutant stores.
A future maintainer adds parallelism without realizing the env-var is process-global, breaking isolation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `REBAR_SYNC_PULL=off` (or `REBAR_NO_SYNC=1`) alongside `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off` in the `run_e2_benchmark.py` harness environment and update the isolation AC, gate-execution mechanism paragraph, and `--self-check-isolation` assertion to set and verify both env vars — `ensure_fresh()` pull-sync is controlled by `REBAR_SYNC_PULL`, not `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH`.
evidence: Plan: 'REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off' is listed as the sole sync guard in the review_plan call and the isolation mechanism paragraph, with no mention of REBAR_SYNC_PULL=off
src/rebar/_reads.py:12-16 explicitly documents 'Opt out with REBAR_SYNC_PULL=off (permanent alias REBAR_NO_SYNC=1)'
src/rebar/_engine_support/reads.py:186-248 shows ensure_fresh() checks _sync_disabled() which reads REBAR_SYNC_PULL, not REBAR_SYNC_PUSH
src/rebar/_store/sync.py:97: '_git(tracker, "reset", "--hard", remote, "--quiet")' on fast-forward — will overwrite injected mutant events if origin/tickets is ahead of local HEAD
src/rebar/_store/push.py confirms REBAR_SYNC_PUSH only controls outbound push, not inbound fetch/reconverge
findings.finding: The plan asserts REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off is the sufficient isolation guard against the standalone clone mutating the real store, but the actual pull-sync path (ensure_fresh → reconverge → git reset --hard origin/tickets) is governed by a separate env var, REBAR_SYNC_PULL=off, which the plan never mentions. Every review_plan call flows through show_ticket → _reads._fresh(tracker) → reads.ensure_fresh(), which (if REBAR_SYNC_PULL is not disabled) fetches origin/tickets and can reset --hard over injected mutant content before the gate runs.
findings.location: ## What — Gate-execution mechanism paragraph; AC: 'The benchmark run is ISOLATED from the shared store by CONSTRUCTION'; runs/e2_benchmark.py scope entry
scenarios: run_e2_benchmark.py injects a mutant into the standalone clone via rebar edit, then calls review_plan. The first show_ticket/list_tickets call inside the gate triggers ensure_fresh on the tmp store. Because REBAR_SYNC_PULL is not disabled and the clone has an origin pointing to the real repo, ensure_fresh fetches origin/tickets and reset --hard overwrites the injected mutation before any gate criterion runs, causing every mutant to score identically to its control (baseline detection = 0 for all classes).
Even if reconverge fails softly (network down), the unpredictable fetch-then-reset window introduces a race: mutant detection becomes non-deterministic depending on git fetch latency.
findings.suggested_fix: In run_e2_benchmark.py, set both os.environ['REBAR_SYNC_PUSH'] = 'off' AND os.environ['REBAR_SYNC_PULL'] = 'off' (equivalently REBAR_NO_SYNC=1) before any review_plan call. Update the ## What gate-execution mechanism paragraph and the isolation AC to name both env vars. The --self-check-isolation flag should assert both are set before injection.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `REBAR_SYNC_PULL=off` (or `REBAR_NO_SYNC=1`) alongside `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off` in the harness's environment setup so `ensure_fresh()` skips the inbound fetch+reconverge on the tmp mutant clone, preventing a `git reset --hard origin/tickets` from overwriting injected mutation commits during a `review_plan()` call.
evidence: Plan specifies: `os.environ["REBAR_SYNC_PUSH"] = "off"` but no `REBAR_SYNC_PULL=off`
src/rebar/_reads.py:37-39: `_fresh(tracker)` called unconditionally in `show_ticket()`
src/rebar/_engine_support/reads.py:197: `if no_sync or _sync_disabled(os.path.dirname(os.path.realpath(tracker))): return` — skips sync only when config says off
src/rebar/_store/sync.py:95-98: related-history path with no local commits ahead issues `git reset --hard origin/tickets`
Prior review cache fa6cf7de7c5123e3e6a9 names this exact risk: 'harness clone-isolation mechanism is vulnerable to ensure_fresh auto-reconverge overwriting injected mutation commits'
findings.finding: The harness sets `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off` to block outbound writes but never sets `REBAR_SYNC_PULL=off` (or `REBAR_NO_SYNC=1`) to disable inbound freshness. Every `review_plan()` call flows through `_reads.show_ticket()` → `_fresh(tracker)` → `ensure_fresh()` (`src/rebar/_reads.py:37-39`; `src/rebar/_engine_support/reads.py:186-251`). If the tmp clone's parent directory has no rebar config with `sync.pull=off`, `_sync_disabled` returns `False` and `ensure_fresh` attempts `git fetch origin tickets` + `_store_sync.reconverge()`. For a `--local` clone, `origin` is the real repo; if the clone is not locally ahead at fetch time (possible between `git clone` and the first `rebar edit` injection), the reconverge path issues `git reset --hard origin/tickets` — overwriting injected mutation commits on the clone's HEAD during the very gate call that should be measuring the mutant. This silently destroys mutation isolation without the ISOLATION-OK assertion catching it (that check only verifies the REAL store's HEAD, not the clone's).
findings.location: ## What — Gate-execution mechanism; run_e2_benchmark.py harness env setup
scenarios: Harness clones repo, then calls `review_plan()` before `rebar edit` completes injection — clone is not yet locally ahead, reconverge does fast-forward reset to real origin/tickets, erasing the pending mutation

Even after injection: the 1-minute throttle window means back-to-back gate calls within 60 s skip reconverge, but the FIRST call in each window can trigger it; if the real origin/tickets has advanced since clone creation, `_do_reconverge` may determine the clone is behind and reset
findings.suggested_fix:
In `run_e2_benchmark.py`, set both env vars before any gate call:
```python
os.environ["REBAR_SYNC_PUSH"] = "off"
os.environ["REBAR_SYNC_PULL"] = "off"  # disables ensure_fresh() fetch+reconverge on the clone
```
Document both in the AC proving command and in the README isolation section.

findings.finding: The plan asserts `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off` is sufficient for store isolation, but does not address `REBAR_SYNC_PULL`. The `_reads.show_ticket()` path calls `_fresh(tracker)` → `ensure_fresh(tracker)`, which performs a throttled `git fetch origin tickets` + reconverge on the tmp clone. Because the clone's `origin` is the real repo, an `ensure_fresh` triggered during a `review_plan()` call could overwrite injected mutations (committed into the clone by `rebar edit`) before the gate reads them, silently reverting the mutation and causing the mutant to be reviewed as its control.
findings.location: ## What — Gate-execution mechanism paragraph; `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH = 'off'` line in the harness code block; AC #4 (`run_e2_benchmark.py` runs via verified mechanism)
findings.suggested_fix: In the harness code block and in the mechanism description, set both: `os.environ["REBAR_SYNC_PUSH"] = "off"` and `os.environ["REBAR_SYNC_PULL"] = "off"` (or equivalently `REBAR_NO_SYNC=1`). Update AC #4's prove command to assert both vars are present in the run environment. Also update the README isolation section to document both directions of sync suppression.

findings.finding: The gate-execution mechanism sets `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off` but omits `REBAR_SYNC_PULL=off`, so every `review_plan` call on a mutant/control triggers `ensure_fresh()` → `git fetch origin tickets` on the tmp clone (whose `origin` points back to the real repo). This means each benchmark gate run makes a real network/disk fetch against the real repo, and the clone can diverge from its initial state if the real `tickets` branch advances mid-run — introducing non-reproducibility in the mutant store state.
findings.location: ## What — Gate-execution mechanism (verified) / `run_e2_benchmark.py` description; `src/rebar/_reads.py:37-39` (`ensure_fresh` called on every `show_ticket`/`list_tickets`)
findings.suggested_fix: In the harness's gate-execution environment setup, add `os.environ['REBAR_SYNC_PULL'] = 'off'` (or `os.environ['REBAR_NO_SYNC'] = '1'`) alongside `os.environ['REBAR_SYNC_PUSH'] = 'off'`. Update the AC's `review_plan` invocation description to include this env var. This is a one-line addition that makes the isolation complete.

findings.finding: The gate-execution mechanism sets `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off` to prevent outbound writes but does not suppress the inbound pull path (`REBAR_SYNC_PULL=off`), leaving `ensure_fresh()` able to `git fetch origin tickets` on the tmp mutant clone during each `review_plan()` call — a processing sub-step with no specified fallback or suppression.
findings.location: ## What — Gate-execution mechanism (verified) section; ## Scope — `run_e2_benchmark.py` bullet
findings.suggested_fix: In the gate-execution env block, add `os.environ["REBAR_SYNC_PULL"] = "off"` next to `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off`. Document: 'both REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off and REBAR_SYNC_PULL=off are set so no git fetch or push touches the real store or the clone's remote-tracking refs during any review_plan() call.'

findings.finding: `classify_pair`'s criteria-matching predicate — the rule that decides whether a gate finding's `criteria` list belongs to the `target_criteria` family — is never specified, leaving the plan's entire detection-recall attribution mechanism under-defined for implementers.
findings.location: ## What — AMBIGUOUS / INDETERMINATE verdict handling section; `harnesses/e2_counting.py` specification
findings.suggested_fix: In the AMBIGUOUS/INDETERMINATE verdict handling section, add: 'A finding is **in target-family** iff `set(finding["criteria"]) & set(target_criteria)` is non-empty (set intersection — a multi-criteria finding is attributed to any class whose target set overlaps).'

findings.finding: classify_pair's criteria-matching rule (how a finding's criteria[] list is tested against target_criteria) is never defined, leaving the attribution logic — the plan's entire detection-recall mechanism — unspecified for implementers.
findings.location: ## What — AMBIGUOUS/INDETERMINATE verdict handling section; e2_counting.py specification
findings.suggested_fix: In the classify_pair spec: 'target-family match = set(finding["criteria"]) & set(target_criteria) is non-empty (intersection — a finding citing E4+G6 is attributed to a class whose target_criteria includes E4)'. Add a fixture: finding with criteria=['E4','G1G2'], target_criteria=['E4'] → detected.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
